The Origin

Terroir, intention,
and expertise.

Hacienda Labu cacao grows under the shade of plantation teak on Nicaragua's North Caribbean Coast — a humid tropical microclimate with 2,400–3,000 mm of annual rainfall and an average temperature of 28°C. These conditions, unique in Central America, produce beans of exceptional flavour complexity across a diverse genetic base.

Our post-harvest team operates a modern processing facility developed in close collaboration with internationally recognised cacao consultants. Fermentation and drying protocols are proprietary, refined specifically for our RACCN terroir — available to qualified buyers under NDA.

🌿
Shade-Grown Asocio System
Cacao grown beneath the teak canopy — natural humidity regulation, reduced heat stress, lower pest pressure. The microclimate behind the flavour.
🧬
Rich Genetic Diversity
ICS, EET, UF, IMC, CATIE, and Native Criollo varieties — each contributing distinct flavour character across our two named profiles.
🏭
Modern Processing Facility
Purpose-built post-harvest facility with fermentation boxes, covered drying beds, and QC laboratory. Highly experienced team with decades of expertise in fine flavour cacao.
✅
Rainforest Alliance Certified
Annual independent audit — sustainable agriculture, fair labour, environmental protection. Very low to no cadmium. Very low to no pesticide residues. Full traceability from pod to bag.

The Asocio System
Why shade-grown
changes everything.

The asocio method — cacao planted beneath the canopy of plantation teak — creates a stable microclimate that allows slower pod development, deeper flavour complexity, and reduced pest pressure. No other operation in Nicaragua is doing this at commercial scale.

28°CAverage temperature — stable humid tropical climate, 2,400–3,000 mm rainfall
6+Genetic varieties — ICS, EET, UF, IMC, CATIE, Native Criollo
NDAPost-harvest protocols — proprietary, available to qualified buyers under NDA
Bean Profiles

Two named expressions
and one built for you.

Each profile is named after a river or place within Hacienda Labu's territory — names rooted in Mayangna and Miskito indigenous language. Each represents a distinct genetic blend processed under one of our proprietary post-harvest protocols.

A
Labu
90% Fermentation
Tasting Notes
Very balanced cocoa mass. Strong cocoa note, pleasantly mild. Good roasted note and nutty aromas.
Varieties
ICS · EET · UF · IMC
Origin
Named after the Labu River — Mayangna "Labuni" meaning Twists or Turns

Post-Harvest · 5,000 m² Facility
From pod to bag.
Precision at every step.

Our modern processing facility handles every step from pod break to export-ready bag. Purpose-built, staffed by a highly experienced team, supported by internationally recognised consultants in fine flavour cacao agronomy, soil science, and sensory evaluation.

The Process

Five steps from
harvest to export.

01
Harvest & Pod Break
Selective hand-picking at peak ripeness. Main harvest March–July. Secondary harvest December–February. Pods broken and beans separated from pulp immediately.
02
Fermentation
6–10 days in 500 kg wooden boxes covered with jute sacking. Beans turned every 24 hours to ensure even fermentation. Profile-specific protocols applied — 85–90% fermentation index achieved.
03
Natural Slow-Drying
8–13 days on raised wooden drying beds. Begins at 10 cm layer depth for 3 days, then reduced to 5 cm. Drying time varies with humidity, sunlight, and desired profile.
04
Quality Control
Cut test analysis, moisture measurement, sensory evaluation by trained panel. Very low to no cadmium. Very low to no pesticide residues.
05
Export Preparation
Standard 60 kg jute bags. Moisture ≤7% confirmed at dispatch. Full Rainforest Alliance documentation, phytosanitary certificate, and origin traceability. FOB Corinto — 30–50 day delivery.
Pods at Harvest
Fresh Beans in Pod
Fermentation
Ready for Export
Advisory Team
Philippe Bastide
Agronomy & Technical
15+ years as a technical consultant through CIRAD — the French agricultural research centre for international development. Specialist in fine flavour agronomy and terroir expression.
Egbert Spaans
Precision Agriculture & Soil
14 years as a precision agriculture and soil management consultant. Ensures Hacienda Labu's soil health supports consistently high-quality fine flavour beans.
Raymond Mayors
Sensory & Quality
International specialist in fine flavour cacao sensory evaluation and quality development. Works with Hacienda Labu on sensory profiling across all bean profiles.

Is your cacao Rainforest Alliance certified?
Yes. Rainforest Alliance (UTZ standard) — annual independent audit covering sustainable agriculture, fair labour, and environmental protection.
Can I get a sample?
Yes. Use the form below. We work with qualified buyers on sample programmes before commercial orders.
What is your fermentation process?
Proprietary — developed in-house with international consultants. Full details available to qualified buyers under NDA.
Can you develop a custom flavour profile?
Yes. We work closely with buyers to co-develop profiles tailored to specific production needs. Contact us to begin a conversation.
What are your MOQs and pricing?
Pricing is NY futures base + sustainability premium. Contact us for current pricing and available volume per profile.
Is this cacao organic?
Not organic. Rainforest Alliance certified, 100% traceable from our own estate, very low to no cadmium, no pesticide residues.
